title: Whakawhanake Pūrere Awa / Developing River Monitoring Tools
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will engage with the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu that flows through our rohe. Historically a vital source of kai (food) and wai (water), the awa now faces challenges from urban development and agricultural runoff. This lesson connects technology practice to the Māori concept of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) and the responsibility to protect and enhance our natural environment.
learningObjectives: Design and prototype a simple, culturally-informed water testing tool or monitoring device for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Analyze and present water quality data collected from the awa using both traditional mātauranga (observational knowledge) and modern technological methods.
Collaborate with local kaitiaki (environmental guardians) to understand the historical significance of the awa and identify key technological needs for its protection.

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero (A Walk, A Story)
activities.description: A guided hīkoi (walk) along a section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will learn the pūrākau (story) of the awa, its traditional uses, names of significant bends or pools, and observe its current state firsthand, noting any signs of pollution or health.

activities.title: Te Tirohanga Ā-Ipurangi (The Digital Lens)
activities.description: Students will use digital tablets or smartphones with GPS and camera functions to digitally map and document their observations from the hīkoi. They will geotag photos of concerning sites (e.g., potential erosion, discoloured water) and points of cultural significance, creating a digital journal of the awa's current state.

activities.title: Te Huringa Whakaaro (The Brainstorm)
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students will analyze their digital journals and data. In small groups, they will brainstorm a simple technological outcome (a tool, device, or app concept) that could help monitor the health of the awa or address a specific issue they identified, ensuring their ideas are grounded in the principle of kaitiakitanga.

activities.title: Te Whanaungatanga ki te Hapori (Connecting with the Community)
activities.description: Groups will present their initial technological concepts to a panel including the local kaitiaki and a technology expert (e.g., an environmental scientist or engineer). They will receive feedback on the feasibility, cultural appropriateness, and potential effectiveness of their proposed outcome.

activities.title: Te Mahi Auaha (The Creative Build)
activities.description: Using a provided 'kete of materials' (e.g., recyclables, simple sensors, micro:bits, coding software), student groups will build a low-fidelity prototype or a detailed design of their proposed technological outcome, ready to present and explain its function.

resources: A local kaumātua or kaitiaki from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi
Tablets/smartphones with GPS and camera capabilities
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' monitoring reports and water quality data sets for the Mangakōtukutuku catchment
A 'kete' of prototyping materials: craft supplies, recyclables, simple water testing kits (pH strips, turbidity tubes), and access to block-based coding platforms for digital concepts

tasks: A group portfolio containing their digital journal from the hīkoi, brainstorm notes, and design sketches.
A 3-minute group presentation explaining their prototype, how it addresses an issue facing the awa, and how it embodies kaitiakitanga.
An individual written reflection on what they learned about the connection between technology, culture, and environmental stewardship.

nzcAlignment: Technology: Technological Practice (Level 4) - Brief development, planning for practice, outcome development and evaluation.
Technology: Nature of Technology (Level 4) - Understanding how technological development expands human possibilities and how technology reflects the values of the society in which it is developed.
Social Sciences: Place and Environment (Level 4) - Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges.
